result:
The first thing I notice is the ticking, soft and arrhythmic, like the heartbeat of a house still learning how to be a home. It is the metronome of the refrigerator, the slow drip in the bathroom sink, the click of expanding floorboards that have spent the night forgetting the weight of yesterday’s feet. I lie still so the day will not notice me and demand my name, my plans, my obedience. For a moment I am only a shape beneath blankets, an extra pillow, a rumple in the topography of bedding. Gravity feels negotiable; if I flatten myself enough I might slip through the weave of the sheet and pool on the floor like warm water, evaporate before anyone walks in to ask what’s for breakfast.

But the light leaks in sideways, amber at first, then the color of a bruised peach. It prods the dust on the dresser into tiny galaxies that swirl whenever I breathe. I watch them orbit a plastic dinosaur my nephew left weeks ago, its jaws frozen open in a tiny roar that has been trying for centuries to escape resin. I imagine the dinosaur’s last day: the sky tearing open, the asteroid arriving like a certified letter no one ordered, the sudden humility of teeth. How comforting to know that even thunder lizards had mornings they didn’t want to get out of bed.

I decide to become a historian of ordinary things. I record the biography of the left slipper: born in Guangzhou, shipped in darkness across an ocean it could not spell, displayed under sodium lights, bought by a woman who would later cry in the shoe aisle for reasons unrelated to footwear. The slipper has survived three dogs, two moves, one divorce. Its twin disappeared beneath the stove in 2019 and has not yet mustered the courage to emerge. History is written by the victors, and the remaining slipper flaunts its fuzziness like a trophy.

In the kitchen the kettle is a small monk clearing its throat. I pour last night’s cold water into the sink; it carries with it the ghost of pasta, the echo of a dinner that tasted almost like the one my mother used to make when she still believed I would grow up to be brave. I refill the kettle, watch the bubbles form constellations on the bottom: Orion’s Belt, the Big Dipper, the outline of a country that no longer exists on maps. When the water begins its anxious dance I drop in a teabag labeled “Calm.” The paper tag says “You are exactly where you need to be,” which is the kind of lie we pay herbs to tell us.

While the tea steeps I open the fridge like a confession booth. Inside: a jar of pickles older than some friendships, hummus wearing a winter coat of frost, a single beet wrapped in foil like a clandestine jewel. I close the door without taking anything; sometimes absolution is simply acknowledging the condiments you have neglected. My phone buzzes with headlines—war somewhere, breakthrough somewhere else, a celebrity who has finally said the thing everyone will quote until the next thing. I scroll with the detachment of a god who lost interest halfway through Genesis. The screen reflects my face: a moon waxing toward hunger.

I carry the mug to the window where the city is assembling itself. Delivery scooters braid through traffic like metallic fish. A man in a green vest sweeps the sidewalk with the tenderness of a grandfather combing a child’s hair. Across the street Mrs. Chen is watering the flowers on her fire escape; she waves the plastic spout at me the way people once waved handkerchiefs at departing ships. We have never spoken, but every day we perform this semaphore of being alive. I raise my cup in reply. Somewhere inside her apartment I imagine the smell of ginger and scallion, the sound of a game show giving away prizes no one knew they wanted.

I think about the word “ordinary.” How it used to feel like a minor insult, a grade of B- in the exam of existence. Now it tastes like safety. Ordinary is the moment before the phone rings with bad news, the hush between sirens, the stillness of bread cooling on a rack. Ordinary is the quilt your grandmother sewed from dresses she no longer fit into, each square a portal to a version of her you will never meet. I trace the rim of the mug and feel the tiny chip on the lip; it is shaped like the country of my childhood if you squint and believe in annexations of memory.

I decide to write a letter to my future self. Dear You, I begin, then stall, because time is a prankster who forwards all mail to an undisclosed location. Instead I write to the dinosaur: Dear Terrifying Lizard, I’m sorry we turned your bones into museum gossip. I’m sorry we sell plastic effigies of your despair in gift shops. I keep you on my dresser to remind myself that every apex is temporary, that panic is best deployed sparingly, like saffron. I think you would have liked the sunrise; even you, in your last morning, might have paused mid-roar to notice how the light made your shadow stretch like a bridge to whatever came next.

The tea is gone except for the dregs, a brown galaxy spiraling at the bottom. I tip the cup onto a saucer and read the leaves as if they were a map I still believe in. I see: a door left ajar, a pair of shoes walking away, a tree that grows backwards into seed. I see myself waving from a window that isn’t this window, older or younger—impossible to tell when the glass is steam. I see the dinosaur, now life-size, stepping delicately over the clutter of my life, careful not to crush the slippers. It lowers its head and sniffs the beet wrapped in foil. Perhaps even extinction gets nostalgic.

I rinse the cup. The water circles the drain clockwise; I live in the northern hemisphere so this is supposed to mean something about surrender. I dry the cup with a towel that once belonged to someone I loved. The towel no longer smells like him—only lemon detergent and the faint metallic breath of pipes. Ownership is a temporary tattoo; eventually everything returns to the communal drawer of the universe. I fold the towel the way he used to, thirds then halves, a quiet origami that says I remember even when I don’t want to.

The ticking has changed tempo. The refrigerator cycles off, leaving a hush so complete I can hear my blood negotiating intersections. I stand in the middle of the kitchen with no mission beyond witnessing the ellipsis of the day. Later there will be errands, emails, the small heroism of showing up. For now I am a comma between mornings, a pause that admits the sentence is not finished. Outside, a cloud shaped like an apostrophe drifts across the sun, suggesting that even the sky is possessive of its light.

I take the dinosaur from the dresser and carry it to the window. Together we survey the swept sidewalk, the watered flowers, the invisible choreography of lives being lived one breath at a time. I press its tiny foot against the glass, leaving a crescent of condensation—an extinct creature’s footprint on a planet that keeps revolving despite every evidence to the contrary. The cloud moves on; the room brightens. I set the dinosaur on the sill, tail pointing toward whatever comes after this.

In the quiet I hear it: a roar the size of a mustard seed, growing.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
